D1.action: Compare imports against declared dependencies and fail on missing, unused, transitive or misplaced packages.
Scan repos, images and filesystems for secrets, CVEs, misconfigurations and SBOM gaps before release.
Keep the working tree clean after audits; treat historical artifacts as reference material, not active output.

D2.action: Treat missing or stale dependency declarations as completeness debt and block delivery until reconciled.
Keep the operational contract complete by storing state, evidence and checkpoints outside the chat.
Batch predictable authorizations and questions before long runs so the control plane can execute without interruptions.
Keep the knowledge base alive by continuously absorbing lessons from the core project and its satellites.
Keep an explicit uncertainty ledger so protocol docs separate verified facts from assumptions before they become doctrine.

D3.action: Require failure messages that explain the mismatch clearly enough to debug without guesswork.
Use explicit state and evidence fields so the system tells a clear causal story instead of relying on memory.
Prefer descriptive names and reduce structural noise so purpose is visible at first glance.

D4.action: Centralize provider routing and fallbacks so the code does not grow provider-specific branching spaghetti.
Flatten nested structure when it simplifies maintenance and removes needless indirection.
Review graph hubs first when the catalog changes, because high fan-in nodes carry the largest impact radius.

D5.action: Turn failure handling into a structured protocol with next steps, evidence and a visible recovery path.
Make failing assertions explain what to do next so the angry path is actionable, not noisy.
Treat warnings and known non-blocking findings as operational errors until they are fixed or explicitly blocked.

D6.action: Enforce clean boundaries, compact state and explicit handoffs to avoid slop and context drift.
Allow exclusions only when they are minimal, justified and real; do not use theater constructs to simulate progress.

D7.action: Use security scanning as a mandatory gate for secrets, vulnerabilities and IaC misconfigurations.
Observe risky signals during execution, not after the fact, so live monitoring can interrupt damage early.

D8.action: Keep tests high-signal: assertions should discriminate behavior, not merely confirm presence.
Prevent dependency drift from destabilizing the test suite by validating imports before running coverage gates.
Reject fake coverage patterns such as xfail-as-expected, placeholder tests, mocks without intent or broad skips without evidence.
Require each new guard to break a real circularity and reduce the baseline instead of merely naming the problem.
Split broad coverage theater into discriminative checks so one test never pretends to cover many unrelated vices.

D9.action: Preserve assertion quality so tests fail with precise, inspectable output instead of theater.
Prefer discriminative tests over symbolic coverage; if a test cannot fail for the right reason, it is not protecting the system.
Mark non-falsifiable lessons as DOC_ONLY instead of pretending they can be proved automatically.

D10.action: Meter tokens before and during LLM calls so cost is visible before usage grows.
Unify provider routing, fallbacks and telemetry so cost and resilience are handled once.
Treat output trimming, context hygiene and history externalization as a first-class control, not an afterthought.
Convert every warning and findable issue into a tracked operational error until the backlog is clean.
Keep the root workspace clean so historical data lives in archives instead of polluting active context.
Watch token and quality signals live during the run, not only in a post-mortem report.

D11.action: Use security scanning as a pre-merge and pre-release gate for filesystems, images and IaC.
Keep exclusions minimal and auditable so the security posture stays real instead of ceremonial.

D12.action: Fuse satellite learnings into the canonical knowledge base only after normalization and deduplication.
Normalize, deduplicate and record new learnings before folding them into the central knowledge base.
Check shared state and recent commits before editing so concurrent sessions do not overwrite each other silently.
